bibliographic_data.title: Deep 3D Convolutional Neural Network for Automated Lung Cancer Diagnosis
bibliographic_data.abstract: Computer Aided Diagnosis has emerged as an indispensable technique for validating the opinion of radiologists in CT interpretation. This paper presents a deep 3D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture for automated CT scan-based lung cancer detection system. It utilizes three-dimensional spatial information to learn highly discriminative 3D features instead of 2D features like texture or geometric shape, which need to be generated manually. The proposed deep learning method automatically extracts the 3D features on the basis of spatio-temporal statistics. The developed model is end-to-end and is able to predict malignancy of each voxel for given input scan. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of proposed 3D CNN network for classification of lung nodule in spite of limited computational capabilities.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents a deep 3D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture for automated CT scan-based lung cancer detection, utilizing three-dimensional spatial information to learn highly discriminative features.
core_content.key_methodology: A 3D CNN is implemented using Keras API and TensorFlow backend on a CPU platform. The model uses small 48x48x48 cubes extracted from the lung scan volume as input. It consists of two convolutional layers with 32 kernels, followed by max-pooling, then two more convolutional layers with 64 kernels, another max-pooling layer, and finally two convolutional layers with 128 and 256 kernels respectively, before flattening and dense layers. The model is trained on 720 scans, with 80 used for validation and 88 for testing.
outcomes: The proposed 3D CNN network effectively classifies lung nodules despite limited computational capabilities.
It automatically extracts 3D features from raw image data without manual feature extraction or selection.
core_content.full_text_markdown: This paper introduces a deep 3D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for automated CT scan-based lung cancer detection. The authors highlight the importance of Computer Aided Diagnosis in validating radiologists' opinions and discuss the limitations of traditional 2D CNNs, which treat each slice of the CT scan as an individual image, leading to loss of spatial context information. In contrast, their proposed 3D CNN utilizes three-dimensional spatial information to learn highly discriminative features directly from raw images. The model is implemented using Keras API and TensorFlow backend on a CPU platform, with small 48x48x48 cubes extracted from the lung scan volume as input. After preprocessing, which includes resampling and normalization, the network predicts malignancy of each voxel for given input scans. Simulation results demonstrate its effectiveness in classifying lung nodules despite limited computational resources.
